content: The orthographic and linguistic origin of "reflexion" (the older variant spelling of the common term "reflection" taught in high school English curricula) suggests that LLM self-reflection mechanisms can borrow the idea of accepting dual valid variants of a core concept to improve their flexibility: instead of forcing an LLM to only reflect on a single "correct" problem-solving path, it can treat multiple equally valid reasoning trajectories as dual legitimate spellings of the same end goal, enabling more robust self-critique. This stands in contrast to existing self-reflection methods that prioritize contrasting inconsistent perspectives to weed out errors.
